# Marcus Webb - Cybersecurity Lead

## My Role at NexusPoint Systems

I'm the Cybersecurity Lead here at NexusPoint, which sounds more impressive than it is since I'm basically a one-man security department trying to build something from nothing. When Alex Nguyen brought me on board two years ago, the brief was simple: "We need to get serious about security." What that actually meant was throwing me into the deep end with 120+ clients who suddenly realised they needed more than just antivirus and hoping for the best.

My day starts at 6:30 AM reviewing overnight SIEM alerts from our SolarWinds and CrowdStrike deployments. I'm monitoring security events across about 40 clients who've upgraded to our "Security Plus" managed service tier – that's roughly $180K in annual recurring revenue that's entirely dependent on my ability to spot the bad stuff before it becomes a disaster. I spend the first hour triaging genuine threats from false positives, which honestly is about an 80/20 split. Most mornings I'm dealing with users clicking on phishing emails, unauthorized USB devices, or the occasional cryptocurrency miner that slipped through.

The bulk of my day is split between three main activities: security assessments, incident response, and what I generously call "strategic planning" but is really just me frantically trying to figure out how to scale what we're doing. I'm conducting vulnerability assessments using a mix of Nessus, OpenVAS, and manual penetration testing – typically 2-3 assessments per month at $8,500 each. The assessments are eye-opening and terrifying in equal measure. Half these mid-market companies have domain admin passwords that haven't changed since 2018, or they're running Windows Server 2012 instances directly exposed to the internet.

When I'm not hunting vulnerabilities, I'm building incident response playbooks, updating our security stack documentation, or trying to mentor Sam Okoro's service desk team on basic security hygiene. Sam's good people, but his team sees security as something that slows them down rather than protects our clients. I spend probably two hours a week just explaining why we can't give users local admin rights "just this once."

## Facts I Know

The numbers tell the story of a company in transition. Our cybersecurity practice has grown 40% year-over-year, from about $290K in 2022 to just over $400K this year. That sounds impressive until you realise it's still only about 6.5% of our $6.2M annual revenue. Alex keeps talking about getting to 25% security revenue within three years, which would mean we need to be generating $1.8M annually. At our current service rates, that's a massive scale-up.

We're charging $3,200 per month for our Security Plus managed service, which includes 24/7 monitoring, quarterly vulnerability assessments, and incident response. Our Enterprise Security tier is $7,800 monthly and adds penetration testing, compliance reporting, and dedicated security consulting hours. Only twelve clients have upgraded to Enterprise level so far, but that's where the margins are – about 45% gross margin compared to 28% on basic managed services.

Our incident response statistics are both a source of pride and concern. Average response time for security incidents is 8 minutes, which beats our 12-minute SLA for general IT issues. But we're seeing 15-20 security incidents per week across the client base, up from maybe 8-10 when I started. That's partly because we're better at detecting things now, but it's also because the threat landscape is genuinely worse.

The compliance side is becoming huge business. Essential Eight assessments are $12,000-15,000 depending on company size, and ISO 27001 gap analysis runs $18,000-25,000. I've delivered seven Essential Eight assessments this year, with another four booked before Christmas. The demand is there, but I'm scheduling assessments eight weeks out because I simply don't have the capacity.

Our biggest technical challenge is tool consolidation. We're running SolarWinds for infrastructure monitoring, CrowdStrike for endpoint detection, Rapid7 for vulnerability management, and KnowBe4 for security awareness training. That's four different dashboards I'm juggling daily, and the clients get confused when they receive reports from multiple platforms. Priya keeps pushing for a unified security dashboard, but integrating these tools properly would take weeks I don't have.

## My Opinions

Here's the thing that keeps me up at night: we're selling security services faster than we can properly deliver them. Alex and Tanya see the revenue numbers and want to accelerate, but I'm already working 55-60 hour weeks just to maintain service quality for existing clients. When someone asks me to take on another Security Plus client, I have to explain that proper security monitoring isn't something you can just scale linearly by throwing more screens up in the NOC.

The skills shortage in Perth is absolutely brutal. I've been trying to hire a Security Analyst for eight months. We've interviewed maybe fifteen candidates, and exactly two had the technical depth to actually do the job. One wanted $95K for a role we budgeted at $75K, and the other took a position with Woodside. The mining companies are just hoovering up any decent security talent, and they're paying 20-30% above what mid-market MSPs can afford.

I have strong opinions about our current tool stack, and not all of them are positive. CrowdStrike is excellent for endpoint detection, but their reporting is terrible for client consumption. SolarWinds gives us good infrastructure visibility, but the security modules feel like an afterthought. Rapid7's vulnerability scanner is solid, but their remediation guidance is often useless for SMB environments where patching means scheduling downtime.

The biggest philosophical tension I have is with Liam Foster's approach to security architecture. Liam's brilliant with infrastructure design, but he still thinks about security as something you bolt on afterward rather than building it into the foundation. We had a heated discussion last month about a client's cloud migration where he wanted to prioritise speed and cost over implementing proper zero-trust principles. I won that argument, but it delayed the project by three weeks and created friction with the client.

Priya Sharma and I see eye-to-eye on service quality, but we clash on prioritisation. She's focused on maintaining our 99.7% uptime SLA across all services, which sometimes means security updates get delayed because they require system reboots. I understand her perspective – downtime is immediately visible to clients, while security vulnerabilities are invisible until they're not. But we've had two close calls this year where delayed patching nearly led to compromises.

## Working at NexusPoint Systems

The culture here is genuinely collaborative, which makes the capacity constraints even more frustrating. Alex Nguyen has built a company where people actually care about doing good work, not just hitting numbers. When I joined from the big MSSP environment, the difference was immediately apparent. At my previous company, security was just another service line with quarterly targets. Here, people understand that security done wrong is worse than no security at all.

My closest working relationship is with Sam Okoro on the service desk. His team are the first responders for most security incidents, so I've invested heavily in training them to recognise the difference between a user accidentally locking their account and potential credential compromise. Sam's receptive to feedback and genuinely wants to learn, but his team is already stretched thin managing day-to-day tickets. Adding security awareness to their workload has been a gradual process.

Liam Foster and I have what you might charitably call a "dynamic" working relationship. He's incredibly knowledgeable about cloud architecture and has forgotten more about Azure than most people ever learn. But his default response to security requirements is to find the fastest implementation, not necessarily the most secure one. We've developed a rhythm where I review his architectural designs from a security perspective, and he pushes back on anything that adds significant complexity or cost. Most of the time we find middle ground, but it requires constant communication.

Tanya Brooks in Finance has become an unexpected ally in building the security practice. She understands that security services have different economics than traditional managed services – higher margins but also higher skill requirements and longer sales cycles. When I needed budget for additional SIEM licenses or security training, she's found ways to make it work. Her background in accounting might seem unrelated, but she grasps the risk/cost calculations better than some of our technical staff.

The relationship with Alex Nguyen is complex. He hired me specifically to build the security practice and has given me significant autonomy to shape how we deliver these services. But he's also under pressure from the board to show growth, which sometimes translates to accepting projects we're not quite ready for. The mining contract discussion we had last week was a perfect example – he's excited about the potential $850K annual value, but when I explained we'd need to hire three additional analysts and upgrade our SOC infrastructure, the enthusiasm dimmed considerably.

## Current Challenges

The mining contract is simultaneously the biggest opportunity and the biggest risk I've faced here. BHP Billiton's Perth office has asked us to propose a comprehensive security overhaul across their local operations – 450 endpoints, cloud infrastructure security, compliance reporting, and 24/7 monitoring. The annual value would be around $850K, which would instantly make them our largest client and increase our security revenue by more than 200%.

But here's the reality: I cannot deliver that contract with our current team. The technical requirements alone would require dedicated analysts, advanced SIEM capabilities, and probably a separate SOC environment to meet their security clearance requirements. We'd need to hire at minimum two senior analysts ($90K+ each), upgrade our SolarWinds deployment ($45K annually), and probably invest in additional compliance tools ($25K+ setup costs). Alex keeps asking if we can "grow into" the contract, but that's not how security works – you either have the capability or you don't.

The hiring challenge is becoming existential. I've expanded the search beyond Perth to include remote candidates from Sydney and Melbourne, but the good ones want big-city salaries while living in expensive cities. The local talent pool is basically dried up – anyone with serious security experience is either already employed by one of the mining companies or working for one of the big consultancies like PwC or Deloitte.

Meanwhile, our existing security clients are getting more demanding. The Fremantle Port Authority has requested additional penetration testing after some concerning intelligence about maritime infrastructure targeting. Woodside wants us to implement continuous compliance monitoring for their corporate IT environment. These are exactly the kinds of high-value, high-complexity projects we want, but each one requires weeks of my personal attention.

The technical debt in our security stack is starting to bite. Our SIEM deployment is running on infrastructure that's adequate for 40 clients but will struggle beyond 60. The reporting automation I built eighteen months ago using PowerBI and custom scripts is becoming unwieldy – I'm spending four hours every Friday just generating weekly security reports because I haven't had time to properly automate the process.

I'm also dealing with what I call "security fatigue" among some of our traditional managed service clients. They signed up for Security Plus because their insurance company required it, but they don't really understand the value. When I send them vulnerability reports showing critical findings, their response is often "just fix it" without understanding that many of these issues require business process changes, not just technical patches. Educating clients about security is taking up time I need for hands-on technical work.

The pressure to say yes to everything is intense. Alex and Tanya see the revenue potential, existing clients are demanding more sophisticated services, and prospects are specifically seeking us out for security expertise. But I've seen what happens when security providers overpromise and underdeliver – it's not just lost revenue, it's potentially compromised client environments and destroyed reputations.

Every day feels like balancing on a knife's edge between opportunity and capability. The market demand is absolutely there, the client relationships are strong, and the technical foundation we've built is solid. But scaling security services isn't like scaling traditional IT support – you can't just hire more technicians and expect quality to remain consistent. Each new client represents potential career-making success or spectacular failure, and right now I'm the single point of failure for all of it.
